replies.author: @sigurdholbarki8268
replies.text:
It's not just the "Progressive" agenda, part of it is this generation's obsession with trying to be original.

Growing up in the UK we'd regularly hear our actors on chat shows talking about the latest Shakespeare production they'd been in.

They'd talk about the different performances of legendary actors, and each production had different takes, but they didn't change one word in the script.  Whatever elements they emphasised were already in the play to begin with and it was about who could do it best.

One of the problems with this age is studios are always after something "new", whereas in the past our societies could always rely on the oldies to sustain the culture until it was reinvigorated.

People used to understand history was cyclical, but since the 20th century they've started seeing it as linear and progressive.  

Add small p progressive and big P Progressive to the cultural trough we find ourselves in and you've got a recipe for disaster

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
*"What makes me so confused is as an author how do you not just use the actual book as the script?"*
Have you ever read a script? If you have, I'm not sure how you would ask why books aren't just used as the scripts. A novel is written specifically to tell a story, while a script / screenplay is written to provide instructions for how to tell the story. The two formats aren't directly interchangeable. 

*"It's all literally right there! "*
It's not, though. There's actually a lot of conversion required to translate a book to the screen.

*"The dialog, the actions, the scenes. Why change anything??"*
These things are rarely written in a way that can be directly transferred to the script / screenplay, though. One of the most significant issues is that much of the emotion and motivation driving the action, dialogue and scenes in the book are spelled out in narration and internal monologues, neither of which are very useful in a film or television series. Those things have to be translated into actions and dialogue in the film version, and the actions / dialogue created to make those translations have to remain consistent within the story being told on film. Even in Peter Jackson's _Lord of the Rings_ films, widely considered the best book-to-film adaptation of all time, there are all kinds of changes, many of which are _still_ hated by long-time fans of the books. Because there was no way Jackson could just use the novels as a screenplay. 

I'm not saying that the changes made in many / most of the book-to-film adaptations are good or necessary, and I'm certainly not justifying changes that directly contradict the plots, themes, actions, motivations and characterizations spelled out in the books being used as source material. I'm just saying that the idea a book can be easily adapted for film by just using the book as the script is wrong.

replies.text:
That hasn't changed AT ALL.

Have you not seen the utter s**t shows that were the Doom films?

Uwe Boll, franchise destroyer extraordinaire comes to mind.

Having seen the ScarJo Ghost In The Shell adaptation I knew instantly that ANY Hollywood adaptation of Akira would be a steaming pile of dogs**t, no matter which director was allowed to do it.

Hollywood just isn't configured for adapting any IP faithfully.

It's more the exception to the rule when it's done faithfully.

Even Peter Jackson's LOTR trilogy for all the fans fellate him over it is NOT a faithful adaptation of JRR Tolkien's work.

Even without getting into stupid additions like the Witch King shattering Gandalf the White's staff at Gondor in the extended cuts the whole trilogy has at least one fundamental problem that makes it a terrible adaptation.

As a veteran of WW1 and its horrifying trench warfare meat grinders Tolkien despised the glorification of violence with great vehemence, and that attitude is NOT at all what is displayed in the action scenes of PJ's trilogy.

You can say that you like the film trilogy all you want, but that doesn't make it faithful to the authors artistic intent.

The same goes fro Jurassic Park - one of the landmark examples of CGI in live action motion pictures and one of Spielberg's best works no doubt, but it isn't very faithful to the original novel either (the lawyer is really f**ked over by the script turning him into a coward).

Back when Dark Tower came out, I was called heinous things for thinking Idris Elba was the wrong casting choice. Now? Some of those same people complain even more loudly than I did, about movies they don't even care about. My brother in law told me that I was racist for not wanting Idris Elba as Roland. Now ur complains about the black Little Mermaid lol. I could have lived with Idris Elba as Roland, if they had actually done the books right. 

They ruined so much, and I absolutely don't want any more live actions at all. 

Praying Rune Lords and Sword of Truth, never ever get made into films. (legend of the seeker was pretty lame already, but they could do soooooo much worse)
